notes: United States Membership in the International Sugar Organization, , , and , title I, § 1402, , , 1709, provided that:Pub. L. 110–234, title I, § 1402122 Stat. 981Pub. Elimination of Sugar Quota Allocation of Panama, , , provided that:Pub. L. 101–167, title V, § 562103 Stat. 1241Nov. 21, 1989“(a).—In GeneralNotwithstanding any other provision of law, no sugars, sirups, or molasses that are products of Panama may be imported into the United States after the date of enactment of this Act [] during any period for which a limitation is imposed by authorities provided under any other law on the total quantity of sugars, sirups, and molasses that may be imported into the United States: , That such products may be imported after the beginning of the last week of any quota year if the President certifies that for the entire duration of the quota year, freedom of the press and other constitutional guarantees, including due process of law, have been restored to the Panamanian people.Nov. 21, 1989Provided“(b).—Reallocation of Quota AmountsFor any quota year for which the President does not certify for the entire duration of the quota year, freedom of the press and all other constitutional guarantees, including due process of law, have been restored to the Panamanian people, no later than the last week of such quota year, the United States Trade Representative shall reallocate among other foreign countries (but, primarily, among beneficiary countries of the Caribbean Basin Initiative and Bolivia) the quantity of sugar, sirup, and molasses products of Panama that could have been imported into the United States before the date of enactment of this Act [] under any limitation imposed by other law on the total quantity of sugars, sirups, and molasses that may be imported into the United States during any period: , That no one country may receive more than 20 per centum of such reallocation.Nov. 21, 1989Provided“(c).—CertificationThe provisions of subsections (a) and (b), and the amendments made by subsection (c) of section 571 of the Foreign Operations, Export Financing, and Related Programs, Appropriations Act, 1988 [section 101(e) [title V, § 571] of , formerly set out below], shall cease to apply if the President certifies to Congress pursuant to section 561(a) of this Act [, which is set out as a note under , Foreign Relations and Intercourse].”Pub. L. 100–202section 561(a) of Pub. L. 101–167section 2151 of Title 22Similar provisions were contained in the following prior appropriation acts:, , .Pub. L. 100–461, title V, § 565102 Stat. 2268–42Oct. 1, 1988, , , 1329–175.Pub. L. 100–202, § 101(e) [title V, § 571]101 Stat. 1329–131Dec. 22, 1987

Ex. Ord. No. 12224. Implementation of the International Sugar AgreementEx. Ord. No. 12224, , 45 F.R. 45243, provided:July 1, 1980By the authority vested in me as President of the United States of America by an Act providing for the Implementation of the International Sugar Agreement, 1977, and for Other Purposes (; ) [this chapter] and of the United States Code, it is hereby ordered as follows:P.L. 96–23694 Stat. 336Section 301 of Title 31–101. The functions vested in the President by () are delegated to the United States Trade Representative.Public Law 96–23694 Stat. 3361–102. In carrying out the functions delegated to him, the United States Trade Representative shall consult with the Secretary of Agriculture and the Secretary of State. The United States Trade Representative may, with the consent of the head of another Executive agency, redelegate some or all of those functions to the head of such agency.1–103. This Order is effective .July 1, 1980Jimmy Carter.

Ex. Ord. No. 12224. Implementation of the International Sugar AgreementEx. Ord. No. 12224, , 45 F.R. 45243, provided:July 1, 1980By the authority vested in me as President of the United States of America by an Act providing for the Implementation of the International Sugar Agreement, 1977, and for Other Purposes (; ) [this chapter] and of the United States Code, it is hereby ordered as follows:P.L. 96–23694 Stat. 336Section 301 of Title 31–101. The functions vested in the President by () are delegated to the United States Trade Representative.Public Law 96–23694 Stat. 3361–102. In carrying out the functions delegated to him, the United States Trade Representative shall consult with the Secretary of Agriculture and the Secretary of State. The United States Trade Representative may, with the consent of the head of another Executive agency, redelegate some or all of those functions to the head of such agency.1–103. This Order is effective .July 1, 1980Jimmy Carter.

children.label: A
children.text: the imposition of limitations on the entry of sugar which is the product of foreign countries, territories, or areas not members of the International Sugar Organization, and

children.label: B
children.text: the prohibition of the entry of any shipment or quantity of sugar not accompanied by a valid certificate of contribution or such other documentation as may be required under the Agreement;

require of appropriate persons the keeping of such records, statistics, and other information, and the submission of such reports, relating to the entry, distribution, prices, and consumption of sugar and alternative sweeteners as he may from time to time prescribe; and

take such other action, and issue and enforce such rules or regulations, as he may consider necessary or appropriate in order to implement the rights and obligations of the United States under the Agreement.
